title: Chaos-Gated GitOps Promotion on GKE
summary: The pipeline blocks staging from reaching production. The only way through is surviving real failure - pod kills, network latency, RPC outages - under live load, with real payments processing.
problem: Most pipelines prove that manifests apply. They do not prove that payment-critical workloads still behave correctly when pods die, dependencies slow down, or network conditions degrade during promotion.
approach: Failure testing is a release gate here, not a post-release exercise. Built on ArgoCD, Kargo, Chaos Mesh, and Prometheus - with real payment traffic, not synthetic load.
impact: Created a public reference architecture for reliability-gated promotion, backed by measurable x402/Permit2 latency improvements and a live failure-testing workflow designed to stop fragile releases before production.
proofPoints: No prior public implementation of this Kargo + Chaos Mesh promotion gate pattern exists
Staging is hard-blocked from prod until chaos verification passes - no override, no workaround
x402/Permit2 migration cut end-to-end p95 from 1.8s to 700ms - 2.5x faster, simpler failure modes

title: Kubernetes Supply Chain Security Platform
summary: A Kyverno-based admission control platform enforcing image signing, SBOM validation, and CVE-aware policy gates before workloads reach production.
problem: Teams often bolt supply chain controls onto CI without ensuring the cluster itself rejects untrusted or non-compliant artifacts.
approach: Implemented policy-driven controls with Kyverno, Cosign, Sigstore, and reporting workflows that make trust decisions visible and enforceable inside Kubernetes admission.
impact: Recognized by the Kyverno community as a showcase reference for policy-based supply chain security patterns.
proofPoints: Cluster-level enforcement instead of CI-only checks
Admission policies for signatures, SBOMs, and vulnerability posture

title: AWS Auto-Scale Stack Infrastructure Automation
summary: A production-style three-tier AWS application provisioned with Terraform, configured with Ansible over SSM, and scaled automatically under real load.
problem: Autoscaling demos often stop at provisioning and never show how infrastructure, configuration management, runtime traffic flow, and least-privilege operations work together under load.
approach: Built a VPC, ALB, Auto Scaling Group, RDS MySQL, CloudWatch alarm-based scaling, and an Ansible control flow that runs through AWS Systems Manager instead of SSH. New instances bootstrap themselves at launch through user data and local Ansible execution.
impact: Turned a traditional AWS three-tier stack into a repeatable platform automation project with documented architecture, self-bootstrapping instances, and observable scaling behavior.
proofPoints: CloudWatch alarm-based simple scaling from 2 to 6 app instances under load
Ansible over AWS Systems Manager with no SSH, no port 22, and no bastion
Full architecture documented from Terraform state through runtime traffic path
